title: Hemp seed cold-process soap
subtitle: A conditioning bar built on hemp seed, olive, and coconut oils with a 5% superfat
excerpt: Hemp seed oil is exceptionally high in linoleic acid, which makes this bar one of the most conditioning in the cold-process range. The formula pairs it with olive for creaminess and coconut for a firm, long-lasting bar.
sourceNotes: Formulation derived from standard cold-process soap-making references including Brambleberry and Soap Queen resources. SAP values from SoapCalc. Hemp seed oil linoleic acid content from USDA nutritional database.

recipe.yieldDescription: 1 kg soap batter (~7 x 130 g bars after 4-week cure)
recipe.batchNotes: Scale the batch by multiplying all weights proportionally. Run the adjusted figures through a lye calculator before making.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Bars must cure for a minimum of 4 weeks before use. Cure time improves hardness and gentleness.
recipe.temperatureNote: Combine lye solution and oils when both are between 40 and 45 degrees Celsius.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: 18 months from cure date stored in a cool, dry place. Hemp seed oil is high in linoleic acid and oxidises faster than olive or coconut oil. Discard if the bar smells rancid or shows orange spots (DOS).

glossaryTerms.definition: The percentage of oils left unsaponified in a soap formula. A 5% superfat means 5% of the oils remain as free fatty acids, adding conditioning properties to the finished bar.

The point in cold-process soap making when the oils and lye solution have emulsified enough that a drizzle of batter leaves a visible trail on the surface before sinking. Light trace is ideal for most pours.
Orange or brown spots that appear on cured soap, caused by oils going rancid. Soaps high in unsaturated fatty acids, such as hemp seed oil, are more susceptible.
The chemical reaction between a fat or oil and an alkali (sodium or potassium hydroxide) that produces soap and glycerol. The ratio of alkali to fat is governed by each oil's SAP value.
A reduction in the theoretical amount of lye needed to fully saponify all the oils. A 33% lye discount creates the superfat that conditions the skin and provides a safety margin against excess lye.

content.text: Hemp seed oil is one of the most nourishing oils you can put in a cold-process bar. Its high linoleic acid content makes it particularly well-suited to dry, sensitive, or mature skin types. This formula keeps the hemp seed oil at 25% of the oil weight, balanced by olive for a creamy lather and coconut for a firm bar with good cleansing. The
. Shelf life is 18 months from the end of the cure period. The bar is susceptible to
if stored in warm or humid conditions, so keep cured bars somewhere cool and dry.
This recipe uses sodium hydroxide (NaOH), a caustic alkali. Follow the full
guidance before starting. Wear protective goggles and gloves throughout. Mix lye into water (never water into lye). Keep children and pets out of the room.

items.prepNote: 25% of oil weight; use fresh, well within date
Run through a lye calculator to confirm for your exact oil batch
33% of oil weight; used for lye solution

content.text: technique. In brief: weigh all oils and melt the coconut oil if solid. In a separate heat-resistant jug, carefully pour the sodium hydroxide into the distilled water (not the other way around) and stir until dissolved. The solution will heat sharply. Allow both the oils and the lye solution to cool to 40 to 45 degrees Celsius.
Pour the lye solution slowly into the oils while stirring. Use a stick blender in short bursts, alternating with hand stirring, until
is reached. At light trace, pour into the prepared mould. Cover with a piece of cardboard and wrap in a towel to insulate. Leave undisturbed for 24 to 48 hours.
Unmould and cut into bars. The
process continues during the cure. Cure on a rack with good air circulation for a minimum of 4 weeks. Hemp seed bars benefit from a 6-week cure.

Cured bars keep for 18 months in a cool, dry place away from direct sunlight. Wrap individual bars in paper or store in an open-sided rack. Avoid airtight containers, which trap moisture. Discard any bar that smells rancid, feels slimy, or shows orange or brown spots (DOS). The high linoleic acid content means hemp seed soap has a shorter shelf life than an all-olive or all-coconut bar.
